core.intro: Lemons are the easiest citrus to grow and the most widely planted fruit tree in Australian backyards, happy in a sunny corner of the garden or in a pot on a courtyard in every mainland state. Get three things right, the variety, steady water and feeding, and a watch on a couple of pests, and one tree will keep a kitchen in fruit for most of the year. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Three varieties cover almost every Australian garden. Eureka is nearly thornless and stays a manageable size, and in warm districts it fruits on and off through much of the year, but it is the least cold-hardy of the three. Lisbon is a big, vigorous, thorny tree that takes both heat and cold better than any other lemon and carries a heavy main crop in winter and early spring. Meyer is not a true lemon but a cross between a lemon and an orange: it is the most cold-tolerant, stays compact, and gives thinner-skinned, sweeter, lower-acid fruit, which makes it the pick for pots and cool districts. Beyond the big three you will also see Villa Franca (an Eureka type favoured for summer fruit), the sweet low-acid Lemonade, seedless Eureka selections, the rough-skinned and very hardy bush lemon, the Spanish Fino and Verna, and aromatic Yuzu grown for its juice and zest. Most are sold grafted, and many come in dwarf forms for tubs. Decide on your climate and how you will use the fruit first, then pick a named variety to match.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree is enough
sections.body: Lemons are self-fertile and set fruit without a pollinator, so a single tree crops on its own with no second variety needed. That is part of why a lemon is such an easy first fruit tree, and why one suits a small garden or a courtyard pot. Bees and other insects still visit the blossom, but you do not have to plant a partner tree to get a crop.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give a lemon the sunniest, most sheltered spot you have, out of strong wind and away from frost hollows, in deep, free-draining soil at a slightly acid pH of about 6 to 7. Lemons are very prone to phytophthora collar and root rot in wet ground, so never plant where water pools, and lift the tree onto a low mound if your soil is heavy clay. Set it at the same depth it grew in the pot, keep the graft union at least 15 cm clear of the soil so the rootstock cannot take over, and do not put fertiliser in the planting hole. Container-grown trees can go in through the warmer months as long as you keep the water up (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Lemons are thirsty, hungry trees, and most backyard disappointments trace back to too little of one or the other. Water a young tree every few days through its first summer, and give a bearing tree a deep soaking at least once a week in dry weather, keeping the water up while the fruit is filling. The feeding roots sit in the top 30 cm of soil out to the edge of the canopy, so mulch well to hold moisture but keep the mulch back from the trunk, which otherwise invites collar rot. Citrus need more nitrogen than any other nutrient, and being evergreen they feed across the warmer months rather than in a single hit. The NSW Department of Primary Industries home-garden program is a complete citrus fertiliser of about 10:4:6 NPK at 500 g per year of the tree's age (so roughly 2.5 kg for a five-year-old) up to about 5 kg a year once the tree is ten years or older, applied in late winter so the nitrogen is there for the spring flush, with a 700 g top-up of sulphate of ammonia in November for a heavy-bearing tree. Spread it evenly out to the canopy edge, never against the trunk, and water it in. Ease off the nitrogen in late summer and autumn so the new wood hardens before winter. Lemons are also prone to trace-element shortages, most often magnesium (older leaves yellowing from the edge and leaving a green triangle at the base), zinc and manganese (small, pale, mottled new leaves), which a citrus trace-element spray, or correcting soil pH to between 6 and 7.5 with dolomite or lime, will put right.

sections.heading: Harvest and storing
sections.body: Lemons do not ripen once they are picked, so leave them on the tree until the skin has coloured fully and the fruit feels heavy with juice. The tree is also the best place to store them: ripe lemons hang on for weeks or even months, so you can pick a few at a time as you need them rather than all at once. Twist and pull, or snip the stalk, without tearing the skin, and avoid picking wet fruit. How the crop falls across the year depends on the variety, so a Eureka in a warm coastal garden fruits on and off almost year-round, a Lisbon carries one heavy crop in winter and early spring, and a Meyer fruits mainly in winter.

sections.heading: Pests and problems to watch
sections.body: Three sap-sucking troubles turn up on almost every backyard lemon. Citrus leaf miner tunnels silvery, twisting trails through the soft new leaves in the warm months and curls them; an established tree shrugs this off, but on young trees spray the new flushes with horticultural oil rather than cutting the tree about. Scale insects (small brown or white bumps on stems and leaves) and aphids on the new growth both leave a sticky honeydew that grows the black coating called sooty mould and draws ants that protect them, so treat the scale and aphids with horticultural oil, deal with the ants, and the mould clears on its own. The two pests that need a local plan are fruit fly and citrus gall wasp, which differ from state to state and are covered in the state sections below.

sections.heading: Buying a tree
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling. Lemons do not come true from seed, so a seedling gives unpredictable fruit, grows thorny, and can take many years to bear, while a grafted tree fruits within a couple of years and to type. The rootstock matters as much as the variety: trees on trifoliata or citrange rootstock resist root rot and suit heavier, wetter ground (though Eureka is grafted instead onto rough lemon or Benton citrange), while a dwarfing rootstock keeps a tree small enough for a large pot. Check the label for the variety name and whether it is a full-size or dwarf tree. The in-stock table above shows the named lemons available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until a lemon tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted lemon usually carries its first few fruit within two or three years of planting and crops well by its fourth or fifth year. A tree grown from a pip is a gamble: it can take many years, grows thorny, and may never fruit like its parent, which is why a named, grafted tree is worth the extra cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow a lemon tree in a pot or a small courtyard?
faqs.a: Yes, and it is one of the best fruit trees for it. Choose a Meyer or a dwarf grafted lemon, use a large tub of free-draining mix in full sun, water about twice a week in summer because pots dry out fast, and feed little and often with a gentle citrus food. A potted lemon can also be wheeled under cover through a frosty snap, which is part of why it suits cold districts.

faqs.q: Why is my lemon tree dropping its small fruit?
faqs.a: Some fruit drop after flowering is natural, as the tree sheds more than it can carry. A heavier drop of pea to marble sized fruit in early summer is almost always water stress, so a tree that dried out at or just after flowering will let go of its crop. Water deeply and evenly, mulch to steady the soil moisture, and remember that a young tree often sheds a first crop simply because it is not yet big enough to hold it.

faqs.q: Why is my lemon tree growing well but not fruiting?
faqs.a: A healthy tree that will not fruit is usually just too young, since a grafted lemon takes a couple of years to settle in and start cropping. After that, the common causes are too much shade, a hard frost killing the blossom, or too much nitrogen feeding, which pushes leafy growth at the expense of flowers. Give it full sun, go easier on high-nitrogen fertiliser, and be patient.

WA.intro: Growing lemons in Western Australia. The dry Mediterranean south-west suits citrus well, but WA's strict quarantine shapes where you buy your tree, and a different fruit fly is the one to plan around.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west of WA has the warm, dry, Mediterranean summers that citrus enjoy, and lemons ripen well along the coastal belt that runs from Gingin, north of Perth, down to Albany on the south coast. The Gascoyne around Carnarvon, further north, is a warm, low-rainfall horticultural district that brings citrus on earlier in the season. Water is the main input here, because through the long dry summer a lemon needs regular deep watering to set and hold its crop.

sections.heading: Where lemons grow in WA
sections.body: Backyard lemons thrive right across Perth and the south-west. The main citrus plantings follow the coast from Harvey, south of Perth, through Gingin and Moora to the north, with more in the Perth hills, while Carnarvon in the Gascoyne and Kununurra in the far north grow citrus in much warmer conditions. WA grows only a small share of the national citrus crop, so a locally grown tree is worth seeking out.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's quarantine rules make citrus one of the harder plants to bring in from interstate. Live citrus trees and budwood may enter only if they are permitted on the Western Australian Organism List, and they usually have to be treated and certified first, which is why many eastern-states nurseries simply will not post citrus here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA, and a WA-grown tree avoids the problem altogether.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's big citrus pest is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established right across the state from Esperance to Carnarvon and will sting lemons and other citrus, so most growers bait or net. Unlike the eastern states, WA keeps out Queensland fruit fly, which is a prohibited pest here and is eradicated whenever it is found. Citrus gall wasp is now in most Perth suburbs (though not yet in WA's commercial orchards), so cut out and bin the woody stem swellings in winter before the wasps emerge. Citrus canker, a serious exotic disease, is not present: the 2018 incursion that reached WA was stamped out, and Australia was declared free of it in April 2021.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA lemons follow the national pattern, with the main crop maturing through late autumn and winter (about May to August) and smaller out-of-season pickings through the warmer months, especially in the north where the trees flower almost continuously.

faqs.q: Which lemon variety grows best in Perth?
faqs.a: Eureka and Lisbon both thrive in Perth's warm, dry climate and give heavy crops with summer water, and Lisbon is the tougher of the two in heat. For a tub or a cooler, part-shaded spot, a Meyer is the easy choice. Whichever you pick, keep the water up through summer and feed across the warm months.

faqs.q: Do lemons need a lot of water in Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes. Perth sand drains fast and holds little, so water deeply and regularly through summer, mulch to slow it down, and use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent. Because nutrients wash straight through sand, feed little and often rather than in one big dose.

QLD.intro: Growing lemons in Queensland. Lemons grow readily across the warm state, but the humid coast brings more skin disease, while the drier inland is the citrus heartland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warmth keeps lemons almost everbearing, but humidity is the catch. On the subtropical south-east coast and in the wet tropics, damp conditions favour the fungal diseases melanose and lemon scab, which spot the skin and call for protective sprays, while drier inland districts such as the Central Burnett and Emerald have far fewer fungal problems and cleaner fruit.

sections.heading: Where lemons grow in QLD
sections.body: Queensland is Australia's largest producer of lemons and limes, and the heart of its citrus country is the Central Burnett around Gayndah and Mundubbera, with more around Emerald, Bundaberg, and Mareeba in the far north (mostly limes). Backyard lemons do well from the coast to the tablelands, given a sunny, open spot so air can move through the canopy.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to the state and is the major pest of backyard citrus, so bait, net, and clear up fallen fruit. Citrus gall wasp, also native to Queensland and northern New South Wales, raises woody swellings on the stems that you prune out and bin in winter before the wasps emerge. On the humid coast, damp weather brings the fungal diseases melanose and lemon scab, which blemish the skin and need a protective spray program. Citrus canker, a serious exotic disease, is a prohibited pest: Queensland's past outbreaks were eradicated, and Australia was declared free of it in April 2021.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth makes lemon trees almost everflowering, so the season is long. The main crop matures through late autumn and winter (about May to August), with a useful share of fruit picked between December and April, and Villa Franca in particular is managed for a summer crop here.

faqs.q: Which lemon should I plant on the humid Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Any lemon will grow, but on the damp coast the skin tends to blemish, so choose a vigorous everbearing tree like Eureka or Villa Franca, give it an open, sunny, breezy position, and keep up a protective spray. Gardeners in the drier inland districts have a much easier time and can grow any variety with clean fruit.

faqs.q: How do I deal with citrus gall wasp on my lemon?
faqs.a: The wasp lays into the soft new stems and the tree grows woody lumps around the larvae. The simplest backyard fix is to cut off the galled stems and bin them through winter, before the adult wasps emerge in spring, which breaks the cycle. Go easy on heavy spring nitrogen feeds that push the soft growth the wasp prefers, and hang yellow sticky traps if infestations have been bad.

NSW.intro: Growing lemons in New South Wales. NSW grows more citrus than any other state, from the humid north coast to the irrigated inland, so the right approach depends on your district.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: NSW spans the full citrus range. On the warm, humid north coast lemons flower and fruit almost continuously but pick up more skin spotting, while the hot, dry inland irrigation areas give one strong flowering a year and clean-skinned fruit. The cool tablelands are the limit: lemons are the most frost-tender of the common citrus, and the NSW lemon guide advises against planting them where winter temperatures fall below about minus 4 degrees.

sections.heading: Where lemons grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina, in the Murrumbidgee Irrigation Area around Griffith, Leeton and Hillston, is the largest citrus region in Australia. Citrus also grows through central NSW around Narromine and Forbes, and along the coast around Gosford and Lismore, where Eureka is the classic lemon. For a backyard tree, match the variety to whether you are on the warm coast, the dry inland, or a frosty tableland.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to eastern Queensland and north-eastern NSW and is established across much of the state, so it is the fly to manage on backyard citrus. Citrus gall wasp is also a NSW native, long a pest in the north and now spread south into the Riverina, and lemons are among the trees it hits hardest, so prune out and bin the woody galls in winter. On the humid coast, damp weather brings more lemon scab and other skin blemishes.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm coast a Eureka fruits on and off through much of the year, while inland and in cooler districts the main crop comes in winter and early spring, with Lisbon a reliable winter cropper. Leave the fruit on the tree and pick it as you need it.

faqs.q: Which lemon suits the cooler parts of NSW, such as the tablelands?
faqs.a: In frost-prone tableland districts, grow a Meyer, the most cold-tolerant lemon, ideally against a sunny north-facing wall or in a pot you can move under cover on the coldest nights. Protect young trees through their first winters, and lean on Lisbon over Eureka if you want a true lemon, since Lisbon takes the cold better.

faqs.q: How do I protect my lemons from fruit fly?
faqs.a: Queensland fruit fly stings the ripening fruit and the maggots tunnel inside. Net the whole tree once the fruit sets, hang traps and use a registered bait through the warmer months, and pick up any windfalls promptly to break the breeding cycle. Lemons left to colour on the tree are the most at risk, so do not let fallen fruit lie around.

VIC.intro: Growing lemons in Victoria. Victoria is the coolest of the mainland lemon states: the warm Murray Valley is genuine citrus country, but elsewhere a lemon is a tree for a warm corner or a pot, with Meyer the cold-hardy choice.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Lemons are the most frost-tender of the common citrus, so across much of Victoria they need a warm microclimate to do well. The hot, dry north along the Murray is the exception, with reliable commercial citrus, while in Melbourne and the cooler south a lemon does best against a sunny, north-facing wall or in a pot. Young trees are the most frost-vulnerable, and a hard frost can strip leaves and kill blossom and small fruit.

sections.heading: Where lemons grow in VIC
sections.body: The Murray Valley, spanning north-west Victoria and south-west NSW around Mildura, Robinvale, Swan Hill and Dareton, is the second largest citrus region in Australia, though it grows mostly oranges and mandarins. In the rest of the state, lemons are very much a backyard tree, grown in the warmest, most sheltered spot the garden offers or in a tub on a sunny courtyard.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has become established across the Greater Sunraysia district that takes in Victoria's Murray Valley, and the long-standing pest-free area there ceased in 2024, so fruit-fly baiting and netting are now part of growing citrus in the north. Citrus gall wasp has also spread south into the Sunraysia and Riverina, raising woody galls on lemon stems that are pruned out in winter. Across the cooler south, though, the main limit on a lemon is simply frost.

sections.heading: Frost and cool-climate growing
sections.body: In Melbourne and Victoria's cooler districts, give a lemon the warmest spot you have, ideally backed by a north-facing brick wall that soaks up the day's heat, and protect a young tree through its first couple of winters with frost cloth on the coldest nights. A lemon in a large pot can be wheeled to a sheltered verandah for winter, which is often the most reliable way to crop one in a cold-prone garden.

faqs.q: Can I grow a lemon tree in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Yes. Pick a warm, sunny, wind-sheltered position, ideally against a north-facing wall, or grow the tree in a large pot you can move to a protected spot for winter. A Meyer is the most forgiving choice in Melbourne's cooler weather, and good drainage plus steady summer water will keep it cropping.

faqs.q: Which lemon copes best with frost?
faqs.a: Meyer is the most cold-tolerant lemon, which is why it is the usual pick for Melbourne and other cold-prone gardens, and Lisbon is the hardiest of the true lemons. Even so, no lemon is reliably frost-proof while young, so shelter new trees through their first winters wherever frosts are sharp.
